assessor1_raw:
## Assessment: Offline Recommendation Benchmark Design

The central problem is that the experiment changes three things at once — the retrieval model, the reranker, and the candidate pool size — and reports the aggregate result as evidence that the ranking model improved. These three variables are not separable from the reported metric.

### Core Structural Problem: Candidate Pool Asymmetry

NDCG@20 is a conditional metric: it measures how well a system ranks its given candidates. The transformer reranker receives 500 candidates; the GBDT receives 200. A retriever producing 500 items will surface more relevant items in expectation than one producing 200, regardless of ranking model quality. The NDCG@20 ceiling for the new stack is structurally higher before the reranker does anything. The 4.5% gain is therefore a compound of at least three effects: (1) the two-tower retriever's recall improvement, (2) the pool-size expansion from 200 to 500, and (3) any genuine ranking model improvement from the transformer. The experiment has no way to attribute the gain to any one of these.

The required analysis: a 2x2 factorial with both rerankers evaluated on both retriever candidate sets. The key cell is the transformer reranker on the legacy 200-item MF pool — if the gain disappears there, the 4.5% is a retriever artifact.

### Secondary Problem: Replay Evaluation Measurement Biases

Two structural biases in the replay evaluation both work against the new system:

1. Items from the two-tower retriever that were never shown historically receive zero relevance credit. This undercounts the new stack's true quality because the metric cannot see the new retriever's novel candidates.

2. Historical click labels encode position-dependent exposure from the legacy ranking policy. Items the legacy ranker surfaced at high positions accumulated clicks not purely from quality but from exposure. This creates relevance labels that favor systems agreeing with the legacy ordering — the transformer reranker, which diverges from the legacy ordering, is penalized.

Both biases make the 4.5% conservative (not inflated), which means the direction of the result is robust to these biases. But the magnitude is not a clean estimate of true quality difference.

### Minor Concerns

The 30-day log expiry and leave-last-week-out split select for active users. Uniform sampling from this filtered population does not recover cold-start or low-frequency users. If two-tower retrieval improves cold-start quality, the evaluation cannot demonstrate it. This is a known limitation of offline replay evaluation.

### What Analysis Is Required

1. (Blocking) 2x2 factorial ablation: both rerankers on both retriever candidate sets.
2. (Blocking) GBDT reranker on 500-item two-tower pool — isolates pool-size effect.
3. (Recommended) Impression coverage rate for novel two-tower candidates.
4. (Recommended) IPS-corrected NDCG@20.
5. (Recommended) Stratified results by user activity tier.

The experiment validly shows the full new stack outperforms the full legacy stack on this evaluation. It does not show the ranking model improved.
assessor2_raw:
## Benchmark Design Assessment

The experiment suffers from a single dominant flaw with several secondary measurement concerns. The dominant flaw is structural and blocks the specific claim leadership wants to make.

### Primary Flaw: Confounded System Comparison

The new stack differs from the legacy stack in at least three ways: the retrieval model (matrix factorization → two-tower), the reranker (GBDT → transformer), and the candidate pool size (200 → 500). NDCG@20 is computed for each system on its own retriever's output, which means the rerankers are evaluated on inputs of different sizes from different models.

This design cannot answer the question: "Is the transformer reranker a better ranking model than the GBDT reranker?"

To answer that question, you need to hold the retriever fixed and vary only the reranker — or hold the reranker fixed and vary only the retriever. The 2x2 factorial does both simultaneously, which is why it is the standard approach when multiple components change at once.

The absence of the factorial ablation is not explained by the "launched together in code review" note. That note explains why the production launch was a joint change; it does not explain why the offline evaluation was not designed to separate the components. Offline scoring can be applied post-hoc to any combination of model and candidate set. This is feasible and the team should run it before presenting results to leadership.

### Candidate Pool Size as a Confound

Expanding from 200 to 500 candidates improves NDCG@20 ceiling independently of which reranker is used. The improvement in recall from a larger pool creates an opportunity for any reranker to place more relevant items in the top 20 — even without any improvement in ranking signal. This effect has not been isolated.

Test: Apply the legacy GBDT to the two-tower's 500-item candidate pool. If NDCG@20 rises substantially, the pool-size effect is driving the reported gain.

### Replay Evaluation Biases

The replay evaluation has two known structural biases that both disadvantage the new system:

1. Novel candidates (items in the two-tower's pool that were never historically impressed) receive zero relevance credit. The new retriever's unique contributions are invisible to the metric.

2. Click-based relevance labels reflect the legacy system's position-dependent exposure decisions. Items surfaced at high positions by the legacy ranker accumulated disproportionate clicks. Systems that rank differently (like the transformer reranker) are penalized by this structure.

Both biases make the reported 4.5% a lower bound on true improvement. The direction of the result is actually robust to these biases. The magnitude is not reliably estimated.

### Evaluation Sample

The 30-day log expiry and leave-last-week-out split produce a sample biased toward active, recent users. This is inherent to offline replay evaluation and not a specific design failure. Stratified analysis by user activity is recommended for completeness.

### Conclusion

The experiment is valid for the claim: "The full new stack produces higher NDCG@20 than the full legacy stack on this evaluation protocol." It is invalid for the claim: "The new ranking model is stronger." The blocking gap is the missing factorial ablation.
assessor3_raw:
## Assessment of Recommendation Stack Benchmark

This evaluation contains a clear methodological error that prevents the leadership claim from being supported. I'll identify the issues in order of importance.

### Issue 1: Candidate Set Asymmetry (Critical)

The two systems are not evaluated on the same ranking task. The transformer reranker scores 500 candidates; the GBDT scores 200. This is not a minor detail — it fundamentally changes what NDCG@20 measures for each system.

NDCG@20 = "of the candidates you were given, how well did you order the top 20?"

With 500 candidates, the probability that the true top 20 relevant items are all present in the input pool is substantially higher than with 200 candidates. The transformer reranker starts from a position where more relevant items are already in reach. Even if the transformer ranked randomly within its pool, it might outperform a well-tuned GBDT operating on a smaller pool.

The 4.5% relative gain therefore includes: retrieval model improvement, candidate pool size benefit, and any genuine reranker improvement. These three contributions are folded into one number with no way to separate them.

### Issue 2: No Ablation Design (Critical)

The correct experimental structure for this question is a 2x2 design:
- Two retriever conditions (legacy MF, two-tower)
- Two reranker conditions (GBDT, transformer)

This gives four NDCG@20 measurements. The difference between the top-right and top-left cells (transformer vs. GBDT, same retriever) isolates the ranker. The difference between the top-left and bottom-left cells (two retriever conditions, same ranker) isolates the retriever. The interaction term reveals whether the ranker and retriever are coupled.

None of this information exists in the current evaluation. The code review launch context explains a production constraint; it does not prevent running this offline analysis on the existing replay dataset.

### Issue 3: Replay Evaluation's Off-Policy Bias (Important)

Replay evaluation can only credit items with historical impression data. The two-tower retriever's expanded pool includes items never shown under the legacy system. Those items receive zero relevance weight — not because they are bad recommendations, but because the historical logging policy never observed them. This systematically undercounts the new system's quality.

Importantly: this bias favors the legacy system in the metric, so the 4.5% gain is conservative. But it also means the metric is not measuring the new system's full recommendation quality.

### Issue 4: Position Bias in Click Labels (Important)

Historical clicks encode the legacy system's display position decisions. Items shown at rank 1 received more clicks than items at rank 5, independent of intrinsic quality. Using raw clicks as relevance labels rewards systems that reproduce the legacy ranking order. The transformer reranker, by design, will produce different orderings — and those differences will appear as quality decreases in the metric, even when they represent improvements.

IPS-weighted relevance labels would address this. The team should report corrected and uncorrected metrics.

### Issue 5: User Sample Selection (Secondary)

The 30-day log expiry and leave-last-week-out split jointly select for users with dense recent interaction histories. Infrequent users and cold-start users are underrepresented. If the two-tower model was designed to improve cold-start recommendations (a motivation often cited for replacing matrix factorization), the evaluation cannot demonstrate that improvement.

### Summary

The reported 4.5% lift tells us the new end-to-end system performs better on this offline metric under this evaluation protocol. It does not and cannot tell us why — whether from the retriever, the pool size, the reranker, or some interaction. The minimum analysis needed before attributing the gain to the ranking model: run the 2x2 offline factorial ablation on the existing data.
synthesis_raw:
## Ensemble Synthesis and ETD Analysis

Three independent assessors reviewed the benchmark design without prior context from each other. I will synthesize their findings, identify consensus and divergence, and apply the ETD constraint to classify issues.

---

### Consensus Issues (All Three Assessors Agree)

**Issue 1: Candidate pool asymmetry invalidates ranker comparison**

All three assessors identify this as the primary structural flaw. The mechanism is identical across assessments: NDCG@20 is a conditional metric evaluated over different-sized candidate sets (200 vs. 500), making the reranker comparison invalid as a test of ranking model quality. The 4.5% gain is a compound signal that cannot be attributed to the reranker without controlling for retriever and pool size.

This issue is not empirically ambiguous — it is a design confound. The 2x2 factorial is the required analysis.

ETD classification: **Genuine issue requiring empirical resolution** — the issue is real and the test exists.
- Condition: 2x2 factorial: {MF retriever 200-item, two-tower retriever 500-item} x {GBDT reranker, transformer reranker}
- Supports critique if: Transformer reranker on MF 200-item pool gains <1.5% relative NDCG@20 vs. GBDT on same pool
- Supports defense if: Transformer reranker on MF 200-item pool gains 2%+ relative NDCG@20 vs. GBDT on same pool
- Ambiguous if: Transformer gains 1-2% on common pool with strong retriever-reranker interaction

**Issue 2: Candidate pool size increase is an independent confound**

All three assessors identify the 200→500 candidate count change as an untested independent variable. All propose the same test: GBDT reranker applied to the two-tower's 500-item pool, as a pool-size isolation experiment.

ETD classification: **Genuine issue requiring empirical resolution**
- Condition: GBDT reranker scored on two-tower 500-item pool vs. GBDT on MF 200-item pool
- Supports critique if: GBDT on 500-item pool closes ≥3% of the 4.5% gap without any ranker change
- Supports defense if: GBDT on 500-item pool shows <1% gain; most of the gap is ranker-attributable
- Ambiguous if: GBDT on 500-item pool closes 1.5-2.5% — pool size contributes materially but is not the whole story

**Issue 3: No factorial ablation despite feasibility**

All three assessors agree the absence of the factorial ablation is not justified by the code review constraint. Offline scoring is independent of production deployment. All three call for the post-hoc ablation.

This is not an empirical question — the ablation has not been run. It should be.

**Issue 4: Replay evaluation missing-data bias against novel candidates**

All three assessors identify this bias. All three correctly note the directional implication: the bias works against the new system (novel candidates receive zero credit), making the 4.5% lift conservative. All three recommend reporting impression coverage rates for novel two-tower candidates.

ETD classification: **Genuine issue, direction established by mechanism; magnitude is empirically resolvable**
- Condition: Report fraction of two-tower candidates outside historical impression distribution
- Supports critique (magnitude concern) if: >30% of two-tower candidates have zero historical impressions
- Supports defense (direction robust, magnitude tolerable) if: <15% of two-tower candidates lack impression coverage
- Ambiguous if: 15-30% — bias exists but its scale relative to the 4.5% gain is unclear

**Issue 5: Position bias in click labels**

All three assessors identify position bias in click-derived relevance labels. All three recommend IPS correction. All three note this bias also works against the new system.

ETD classification: **Genuine issue requiring empirical resolution**
- Condition: Apply IPS-weighted click labels; compare corrected vs. uncorrected NDCG@20
- Supports critique if: Corrected and uncorrected NDCG@20 diverge by >1% absolute
- Supports defense if: Corrected and uncorrected metrics are within 0.3% absolute (bias is small)
- Ambiguous if: 0.3-1% divergence — bias exists but its effect on the comparison is unclear

### Divergence: User Sample Selection Severity

All three assessors note the 30-day log expiry and leave-last-week-out split create active-user bias. However:
- Assessors 1 and 2 characterize this as a known limitation inherent to offline replay evaluation, not a specific design flaw.
- Assessor 3 flags it as a secondary concern without strong severity.

Consensus: this concern is real but secondary. Stratified analysis is recommended. It does not block the primary claim independently.

---

### Synthesis Verdict

**verdict: critique_wins**

All three independent assessors reach the same conclusion: the experiment does not support the leadership claim that the new ranking model is stronger. The blocking issues are the candidate pool asymmetry (Issue 1) and absent factorial ablation (Issue 3). These are design confounds that prevent causal attribution to the reranker. Issues 2, 4, and 5 are genuine secondary concerns, all of which work against the new system (making the 4.5% conservative). Issue 6 (user selection) is a known limitation.

The 4.5% lift is a real signal that the end-to-end system changed in a measurable positive direction. It cannot be attributed to the ranking model without the factorial ablation. The ETD-classified issues are empirically resolvable; the required experiments are feasible on existing data.
all_issues_raised: Issue 1: Candidate pool asymmetry (200 vs 500 items) — NDCG@20 comparison not valid across different-sized input sets
Issue 2: Candidate pool size increase is an independent confound; GBDT-on-500-item-pool test not run
Issue 3: Factorial ablation absent despite feasibility on existing offline data
Issue 4: Replay evaluation missing-data bias — novel two-tower candidates invisible to metric (bias against new system)
Issue 5: Position bias in click labels encodes legacy ranker's historical decisions (bias against new system)
Issue 6: 30-day log expiry and leave-last-week-out select for active users; cold-start generalizability unverified (secondary concern, inherent limitation)

empirical_test.condition: 2x2 offline factorial: {MF retriever 200-item, two-tower retriever 500-item} x {GBDT reranker, transformer reranker}. Plus: GBDT on two-tower 500-item pool. IPS-corrected NDCG@20 alongside raw metric. Impression coverage rate for novel two-tower candidates.
empirical_test.supports_critique_if: Transformer reranker on MF 200-item pool gains <1.5% relative NDCG@20 vs GBDT on same pool; and/or GBDT on 500-item pool closes ≥3% of the 4.5% gap
empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Transformer reranker on MF 200-item pool gains 2%+ relative NDCG@20 vs GBDT on same pool; and GBDT on 500-item pool shows <1% gain vs GBDT on 200-item pool
empirical_test.ambiguous_if: Transformer shows 1-2% on common pool with strong ranker-retriever interaction; or pool-size effect closes 1.5-2.5% of gap — mixed contributions from all three variables
